topic: The Dawn of Everything — indigenous Turtle Island critiques shaping the Enlightenment
what: Jonny is reading 'The Dawn of Everything' by David Graeber and David Wengrow. The book argues that the Enlightenment (Jonny said 'renaissance' but the book's case centers on the Enlightenment, late 17th/early 18th c.) was in no small part informed by indigenous critiques of European society — particularly from figures like Kondiaronk (Wendat leader, d.1701) — that made their way back to Europe via missionaries and explorers and genuinely influenced Enlightenment political philosophy about freedom and equality.
why_interesting: This inverts the standard narrative where the 'Enlightenment' is a purely European flowering. If Rousseau and others were partly responding to indigenous political arguments about inequality, then the ideas that shaped western liberal democracy have non-western roots. Connects to the broader pattern of the west absorbing outside influence while claiming internal origin.
source: conversation with Jonny, 2026-04-20 — book: The Dawn of Everything (Graeber & Wengrow)
